instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software systems are abstract and intangible. They are not constrained by the properties of materials, governed by physical laws, or by manufacturing processes. This simplifies software engineering, as there are no natural limits to the potential of software. However, because of the lack of physical constraints, software systems can quickly become extremely complex, difficult to understand, and expensive to change.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: There are many different types of software systems, from simple embedded systems to complex, worldwide information systems. It is pointless to look for universal notations, methods, or techniques for software engineering because different types of software require different approaches. Developing an organizational information system is completely different from developing a controller for a scientific instrument. Neither of these systems has much in common with a graphics-intensive computer game. All of these applications need software engineering; they do not all need the same software engineering techniques.
output: [{"entity": "software engineering", "label": "TOPIC"}, {"entity": "embedded systems", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "information systems", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "software systems", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "software engineering techniques", "label": "METHOD"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software engineering is intended to support professional software development, rather than individual programming. It includes techniques that support program specification, design, and evolution, none of which are normally relevant for personal software development.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Computer programs and associated documentation. Software products may be developed for a particular customer or may be developed for a general market. Good software should deliver the required functionality and performance to the user and should be maintainable, dependable, and usable. Software engineering is an engineering discipline that is concerned with all aspects of software production.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software engineering is an engineering discipline that is concerned with all aspects of software production from the early stages of system specification through to maintaining the system after it has gone into use.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software engineering is not just concerned with the technical processes of software development. It also includes activities such as software project management and the development of tools, methods, and theories to support software production.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Embedded control systems : These are software control systems that control and manage hardware devices. Numerically, there are probably more embedded systems than any other type of system. Examples of embedded systems include the software in a mobile (cell ) phone, software that controls anti-lock braking in a car, and software in a microwave oven to control the cooking process.
output: [{"entity": "embedded control systems", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "embedded systems", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "software contorls systems", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "hardware devices", "label": "OTHER"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Batch processing systems :  These are business systems that are designed to process data in large batches. They process large numbers of individual inputs to create corresponding outputs. Examples of batch systems include periodic billing systems, such as phone billing systems, and salary payment systems.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Entertainment systems : These are systems that are primarily for personal use and which are intended to entertain the user. Most of these systems are games of one kind or another. The quality of the user interaction offered is the most important distinguishing characteristic of entertainment systems.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Systems for modeling and simulation These are systems that are developed by scientists and engineers to model physical processes or situations, which include many, separate, interacting objects. These are often computationally intensive and require high-performance parallel systems for execution.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Data collection systems :  These are systems that collect data from their environment using a set of sensors and send that data to other systems for processing. The software has to interact with sensors and often is installed in a hostile environment such as inside an engine or in a remote location.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Systems of systems : These are systems that are composed of a number of other software systems. Some of these may be generic software products, such as a spreadsheet program. Other systems in the assembly may be specially written for that environment.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Stand -alone applications : These are application systems that run on a local computer, such as a PC. They include all necessary functionality and do not need to be connected to a network. Examples of such applications are office applications on a PC, CAD programs, photo manipulation software, etc.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The waterfall model : This takes the fundamental process activities of specification, development, validation, and evolution and represents them as separate process phases such as requirements specification, software design, implementation, testing, and so on.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Incremental development : This approach interleaves the activities of specification, development, and validation. The system is developed as a series of versions (increments), with each version adding functionality to the previous version.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The waterfall model is consistent with other engineering process models and documentation is produced at each phase. This makes the process visible so managers can monitor progress against the development plan. Its major problem is the inflexible partitioning of the project into distinct stages. Commitments must be made at an early stage in the process, which makes it difficult to respond to changing customer requirements.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The implementation stage of software development is the process of converting a system specification into an executable system. It always involves processes of software design and programming but, if an incremental approach to development is used, may also involve refinement of the software specification.
output: [{"entity": "implementation stage", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "software development", "label": "TOPIC"}, {"entity": "system specification", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "executable system", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "software design", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "programming", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "incremental development", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "software specification", "label": "METHOD"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: A software design is a description of the structure of the software to be implemented, the data models and structures used by the system, the interfaces between system components and, sometimes, the algorithms used. Designers do not arrive at a finished design immediately but develop the design iteratively. They add formality and detail as they develop their design with constant backtracking to correct earlier designs.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: One of the earliest models is the Waterfall model, which follows a linear and sequential process where each phase -requirements, design, development, testing, and deployment -is completed before moving to the next. It works well for projects with clearly defined and stable requirements but lacks flexibility for changes.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The Agile model is iterative and highly flexible, allowing development to occur in small cycles called sprints, making it suitable for projects where requirements frequently change.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The Spiral model combines elements of both iterative and waterfall approaches while emphasizing risk analysis, making it ideal for large and complex projects. Another important model is the V-Model (Verification and Validation), where each development phase is paired with a corresponding testing phase, ensuring high quality and reliability, especially in critical systems.
output: [{"entity": "spiral model", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "waterfall model", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "risk analysis", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "v-model", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "verification and validation", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "testing phase", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "critical systems", "label": "TOPIC"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: the Iterative model focuses on building the system in repeated cycles, gradually improving it with each version, while the Incremental model develops the software in smaller functional parts, delivering usable features step by step.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The Prototype model involves creating a working model of the system early on to gather user feedback and refine requirements before full-scale development, which is helpful when requirements are not clearly understood.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: The DevOps model represents a modern approach that integrates development and operations teams to enable continuous integration, testing, and deployment, ensuring faster and more reliable software delivery. Each SDLC model has its own strengths and is chosen based on project requirements, complexity, and the need for flexibility or control.
output: [{"entity": "devops model", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "continuous integration", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "continuous testing", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "continuous deployment", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "software delivery", "label": "TOPIC"}, {"entity": "sdlc models", "label": "TOPIC"}, {"entity": "project requirements", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "complexity", "label": "CONCEPT"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Requirements Engineering involves gathering, analyzing, and documenting user needs. It ensures that the final software meets customer expectations. Includes functional and non-functional requirements. Clear requirements reduce errors and rework later.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Project Management focuses on planning, scheduling, and managing software projects. Includes resource allocation, risk management, and budgeting. Helps complete projects on time and within cost. Tools like Gantt charts are used.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software Quality Assurance (SQA) ensures that software meets quality standards. It involves process monitoring and testing strategies. Prevents defects rather than just detecting them. Improves reliability and customer satisfaction.
output: [{"entity": "software quality assurance", "label": "TOPIC"}, {"entity": "quality standards", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "process monitoring", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "testing strategies", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "defects", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "reliability", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "customer satisfaction", "label": "CONCEPT"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software Architecture defines the overall structure of the system. Includes components, their relationships, and design patterns. Examples: client-server, microservices architecture. A strong architecture ensures system performance and scalability.

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Risk Management identifies and manages potential project risks. Risks can be technical, financial, or operational. Helps in minimizing project failures. Planning ahead reduces unexpected problems.
output: [{"entity": "risk management", "label": "METHOD"}, {"entity": "project risks", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "technical risks", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "financial risks", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "operational risks", "label": "CONCEPT"}, {"entity": "project failure", "label": "CONCEPT"}]

instruction: Extract entities using ONLY the following labels: TOPIC, CONCEPT, METHOD, ALGORITHM, OTHER.
input: Software Testing checks whether the software works correctly and meets requirements. Types include unit testing, integration testing, and system testing. It helps find bugs before deployment. Ensures reliability and quality of the product. Software design defines the system architecture and structure. It includes high-level design (architecture) and low-level design (modules). Good design improves scalability and maintainability. Tools like UML diagrams are often used.
